1.1: Malachi is the last book of the OT in both the Hebrew canon's traditional order and the Christian canon. It was written ca. 450-430 BCE, after the return from exile, during a period of post-exilic religious laxness. The prophet addresses: priests who offer defiled offerings (1:6-2:9), men who divorce their wives (2:14-16), the community's failure to tithe (3:10), and the skepticism of those who question YHWH's justice (3:13-15). Its six disputation speeches (each opening with a divine claim, then an objection, then YHWH's response) address the demoralization of the restored community. Malachi is the bridge between the OT and the NT: its final oracles (3:1; 4:5-6) are the OT's last prophetic words, pointing directly to John the Baptist and Jesus, so that the NT's opening (Matt 1-3; Mark 1; Luke 1-3) is the direct fulfillment of Malachi's closing.
